interpretive_note: P. Sittius contracted business debts and used procurators/assets to satisfy creditors.

source_letter_id: Hor. Serm. 2.3.18-26; Ps.-Acro in Hor. Serm. 2.3.18
latin_evidence: alii ad reddendum faenus, alii ad locandum faenus
english_evidence: Some came to return interest, others to place money at interest.
interpretive_note: Damasippus' real-estate and art business depended on money-lenders at the Ianus medius.
stress_rationale: no liquidity-stress signal under current rules

latin_evidence: cum centies atque quinquagies sestertii summam in faenore haberet, neque de sorte quemquam debitorum suorum neque de usura appellari a suis passus est
english_evidence: Valerius Maximus says Q. Considius had 150 times 100,000 sesterces out at interest and allowed none of his debtors to be pressed for principal or interest during the Catilinarian crisis.
interpretive_note: Q. Considius had fifteen million sestertii out on loans in 63 BCE and later gained prestige by remission to debtors.

latin_evidence: Magno etiam adiumento nobis Hermogenes potest esse in repraesentando; unde ergo numerato? HS DC exprimes ab Hermogene
english_evidence: Hermogenes can be a major help in ready payment, and Cicero says 600 HS can be obtained through him.
interpretive_note: Cicero may have expected financial help from Clodius Hermogenes in 45 BCE.
stress_rationale: no liquidity-stress signal under current rules

latin_evidence: Vestorius noster me per litteras fecit certiorem ... liberaliter se gerit.
english_evidence: Our Vestorius informed me by letter ... he is behaving liberally.
interpretive_note: Vestorius's liberalitas is retained as evidence for financial help and reciprocity in credit relations.
stress_rationale: no liquidity-stress signal under current rules
